ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef bowl / gyudon entry. The specific combo variant with half boiled egg, spring onion, and miso soup side is not individually coded; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific gyudon combo code. A0CKP (beef and rice dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for gyudon or Japanese beef rice bowl combo.

serving.description: 1 combo set (gyudon bowl + miso soup or drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard gyudon combo set comprising approximately 300 g of beef rice bowl topped with half boiled egg and spring onion, plus approximately 200 g miso soup and 50 g condiment sauce, totalling roughly 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef bowl) — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central ingredient data and HPB Singapore nutrient reference for rice-based mixed dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact combo dish. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted ingredient composition: steamed white rice (~55%), thinly sliced simmered beef (~25%), half boiled egg (~10%), spring onion (~3%), dashi-soy sauce (~5%), and miso soup contribution (~2%). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for beef (NDB 13365), egg (NDB 01129), white rice cooked (NDB 20444), spring onion (NDB 11291), and miso (NDB 16112). HPB Singapore Foodo data for cooked white rice and mixed rice dishes used as cross-reference. Values represent a plausible central estimate for a standard restaurant serving.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (cooked, Japanese-style) and mixed rice-based dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for gyudon combo. GI of 65 is estimated using cooked Japanese white rice as the primary glycemic driver (GI ~65–72 per University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021 Diabetes Care). The protein and fat content from beef and egg moderately attenuate the overall meal GI. A composite meal GI of 65 (medium) is a defensible conservative estimate. GL per serving calculated as (65 × 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 550 g serving) / 100 = approximately 22.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dashi-soy sauce and miso soup
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish as the estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 2310 mg per full combo serving) substantially exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium target of 600–800 mg; requesting reduced sauce and omitting miso soup can lower sodium by an estimated 30–40%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice; moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that this combo delivers approximately 102 g of carbohydrate per full serving with a medium GI of 65 and a glycemic load of approximately 22, which can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; portion reduction, substituting brown rice where available, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and egg yolk; high sodium from soy-based sauce and miso
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the combination of saturated fat (approximately 1.6 g per 100 g) from thinly sliced beef and egg yolk, together with high sodium from the dashi-soy sauce and miso soup, warrants moderation; choosing a smaller portion, requesting less sauce, and limiting frequency to occasional consumption is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Boiled Egg and Spring Onion Gyudon Combo is a Japanese beef rice bowl set meal served at Japanese fast-casual restaurants in Singapore, most notably at Sukiya. The dish consists of a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice topped with thinly sliced beef simmered in a sweet dashi-soy sauce (tare), garnished with a soft half boiled egg (hanjuku tamago) and sliced spring onion (naganegi). The combo is complemented by a sweet and spicy Korean chilli pepper sauce on the side and is served with a choice of miso soup or a beverage. The beef topping is tender and umami-rich with a slightly sweet, savoury glaze; the half boiled egg adds a creamy, runny yolk that enriches the sauce; and the spring onion provides a fresh, mild pungency. The dish is a filling, protein-moderate, carbohydrate-dominant meal. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with 8.2 g protein, 4.5 g fat, and 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. A full combo serving of approximately 550 g delivers roughly 814 kcal and over 100 g of carbohydrate, making portion awareness important for individuals managing blood glucose or weight. Sodium content is high due to the soy-based sauce and miso soup, posing a concern for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), driven primarily by the white rice base. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without explicit certification from the restaurant.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact gyudon combo dish as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components (beef, egg, white rice, spring onion, miso) cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for cooked rice and mixed rice dishes. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using Japanese cooked white rice as the primary glycemic driver. Medium confidence reflects the estimation approach rather than a direct measurement.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; actual values will vary by restaurant, portion size, sauce quantity, and preparation method. Sukiya Singapore and similar chains do not publish full nutrition panels in approved databases. 2. Sodium is estimated conservatively; actual sodium may be higher depending on sauce volume and miso soup formulation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not directly reported for this specific dish; these values carry higher uncertainty. 4. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrient fields carry estimated values derived from component analysis. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; Sukiya Singapore is not listed as a halal-certified chain as of the knowledge cutoff. 6. The cuisine_tags in the input (Japanese Curry, Grilled Chicken Skewers, Fried Chicken, Drinks & Beverages) appear to be restaurant-level tags for Sukiya's broader menu rather than descriptors of this specific dish and have not been carried forward. 7. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate using Japanese cooked white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for gyudon combo exists in approved sources.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Removed menu-style numbering prefix from food_name. Added Japanese native-script local name and romanized form. Built full aliases_by_language structure with consistency check against flat aliases. Estimated nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy component data. Estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database using white rice analogue. Populated all health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance. Wrote LLM description exceeding 200 characters. Verified all ontology match_type values use only allowed strings. Confirmed no -1 sentinel values used; all micronutrient fields carry estimated numeric values. Confirmed no zero-macro issues requiring justification phrases.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency — confirmed all Japanese and romanized entries in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases list. Confirmed Chinese aliases (牛丼コンボ, 半熟卵牛丼) appear in both aliases_by_language.chinese and flat aliases. Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass. Decision: PASS.
